metadata.summary: GraphQL lets clients fetch nested, multi-resource data in a single request against a typed schema. Use DataLoader on every relation field; cap depth and complexity on public endpoints.

content.title: GraphQL Patterns
content.summary: GraphQL lets clients fetch nested, multi-resource data in a single request against a typed schema. The schema (SDL) is the contract; code is derived from it. Every relation field must use DataLoader to prevent N+1 queries. Public endpoints must enforce depth and complexity limits.

why.p: Without DataLoader, every nested field triggers a separate DB query — one list of 100 orders fires 100 user lookups in production. Depth/complexity limits prevent DoS via deeply nested or astronomically large queries. Schema-first development (SDL reviewed before code) gives backend and frontend teams a stable contract to work against concurrently.

list.item: Client needs to fetch nested data (user → orders → items) in a single round-trip with field selection.
Multiple consumers (web, mobile, partner) want different shapes off the same backend without bespoke REST endpoints.
You want a typed schema as the contract between BE and FE generators (codegen, Apollo, urql, Relay).
Real-time fan-out via subscriptions belongs alongside the same query/mutation graph.
Federating multiple services into one supergraph (Apollo Federation, Hot Chocolate, Mercurius).
Simple CRUD on a single resource — REST is shorter, cacheable, and well-tooled.
File upload heavy / streaming binary — multipart REST or gRPC stream is better.
Public, cache-on-CDN read APIs — GET + URL semantics outperform POST /graphql.
Hard p99 latency budgets where resolver fan-out is unpredictable.
Tiny team without bandwidth to operate persisted queries, depth limits, complexity scoring.
N+1 queries are the default unless DataLoader is wired into every relation field.
HTTP caching does not work out of the box (everything is POST /graphql with the same URL).
Public schemas without depth/complexity limits are a free DoS amplifier.
Auth at field level is hand-rolled — easy to forget on a new resolver.
Errors arrive in a `data + errors` envelope; partial success makes status-code monitoring useless.
Subscriptions over websockets re-introduce stateful sessions you thought GraphQL would remove.

section.name: sdl-structure
section.p: Define Query, Mutation, and Subscription root types; group related fields with tags and input types for all mutations.

rule.statement: Define Query, Mutation, and Subscription root types; group related fields with tags and input types for all mutations.
rule.rationale: Input types allow validation, evolution without breaking clients, and consistent mutation signatures.

rule.statement: All list fields use Relay cursor-based pagination (Connection/Edge/PageInfo) instead of offset pagination.
rule.rationale: Offset pagination produces duplicates and gaps when items are inserted/deleted between pages. Cursor pagination is stable under concurrent writes.

section.name: federation
section.p: Federation: in federated graphs, declare entity types with @key in the owning subgraph, and extend with @external in consuming subgraphs.

rule.statement: In federated graphs, declare entity types with @key(fields: "id") in the owning subgraph, and extend with @external in consuming subgraphs.
rule.rationale: Federation allows multiple services to contribute fields to the same type while each service owns its domain.

rule.statement: Every resolver that loads a related entity must use DataLoader. DataLoader instances are scoped per-request in GraphQL context; never module-global.
rule.rationale: A module-global DataLoader leaks data across requests. Without DataLoader, a list of 100 orders fires 100 separate user queries — silent quadratic behavior in prod.

example.note: Module-global loader caches data from one request and serves it to another user's request.

rule.statement: Return errors in the GraphQL errors array with extensions.code as a stable enum string. Clients must never parse the message field for logic.
rule.rationale: message strings change with every release; a stable code enum allows clients to handle specific errors without coupling to prose.

rule.statement: Apply depth limiting (max 8–10) and query complexity limits (max 100–500) on every public GraphQL endpoint.
rule.rationale: Without limits, a single deeply nested or complex query can DoS the server or drain the database connection pool.

rule.statement: Use persisted queries in production: clients send a hash, server resolves to vetted operations. Disable introspection for non-public schemas or gate behind auth.
rule.rationale: Persisted queries close injection and DoS surface in one move. Introspection leaks the full schema to unauthenticated attackers.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive GraphQL changes schema-first: an agent edits the SDL, regenerates types and resolvers, then writes/updates DataLoaders and tests. Treat the SDL diff as the artifact a human reviews — never let an agent autopatch a resolver without showing the schema change first. For federation, partition by subgraph and run schema composition checks (`rover supergraph compose`, `apollo schema:check`) as the gate before merge.

list.item: `/faion` (sdd-batch-orchestrator workflow) — sequence schema edit → codegen → resolver → DataLoader → test as ordered tasks.
`/faion` (knowledge/solo/sdd/sdd/) — quality gate that blocks if depth/complexity limits or DataLoader are missing on new fields.
A `graphql-resolver` task agent (Sonnet) — resolver + DataLoader implementation with paste-in SDL.
A `schema-reviewer` task agent (Opus) — checks for breaking changes, missing pagination, cycles, naming.

section.prompt: You are editing the orders subgraph SDL. Add a `refundedAt: DateTime` field to type Order. Update the Strawberry resolver, add a DataLoader for batch_load_refund_status, regenerate the TS client types, and add one integration test that hits /graphql with the new field. Do NOT add depth or complexity changes. Output: unified diff only.
Run `rover subgraph check orders --schema ./orders.graphql` against the prod supergraph. If breaking, propose a deprecation plan with @deprecated reason and migration timeline. Do not push.

list.item: `rover` — Apollo Federation schema check / publish / compose. Install: `curl -sSL https://rover.apollo.dev/nix/latest | sh` · apollographql.com/docs/rover.
`graphql-codegen` — Generate TS types, hooks, resolver scaffolds. Install: `npm i -D @graphql-codegen/cli` · the-guild.dev/graphql/codegen.
`gql.tada` / `graphql-tag` — Inline typed queries. Install: npm.
`graphql-inspector` — Diff + breaking change detection. Install: `npm i -g @graphql-inspector/cli`.
`graphqurl` (`gq`) — curl-equivalent for GraphQL. Install: `npm i -g graphqurl`.
`clairvoyance` — Introspection-blind schema recovery (red team). Install: `pip install clairvoyance`.
`graphw00f` — Fingerprint server engine + lookup CVEs. Install: `pip install graphw00f`.
`graphql-cop` — Security audit (depth, batching, suggestions). Install: `pip install graphql-cop`.
Apollo GraphOS / Studio — SaaS, agent-friendly via Rover CLI. Federation registry, schema checks, ops metrics.
Hasura — OSS + Cloud, partial agent support via REST-ish admin API. Auto-generates schema from Postgres; agents can add permissions via metadata YAML.
PostGraphile — OSS, agent-friendly via config files. Postgres → GraphQL, plugin-driven.
WunderGraph Cosmo — OSS, agent-friendly via `wgc` CLI. Federation router + registry, self-hostable Apollo alternative.
Stellate — SaaS, agent-friendly via config-as-code. GraphQL CDN + caching (the missing CDN story).
The Guild Hive — SaaS + OSS, agent-friendly. Schema registry + usage analytics.
Apollo Sandbox / GraphiQL — OSS, manual. Used for dev; agents can hit `/graphql` directly with curl.

section.name: smoke-check
section.p: Quick smoke check that DataLoader is wired (drop in CI):

example.title: N+1 risk smoke check
example.code: #!/usr/bin/env bash # fail if any *Resolver.ts/py touches db inside a loop without a loader set -euo pipefail grep -rEn 'for .* in .*:\s*$' resolvers/ | while read -r hit; do file=${hit%%:*} if grep -q 'await.*\.find\|\.query\|session\.execute' "$file" \ && ! grep -q 'DataLoader\|dataloader' "$file"; then echo "N+1 risk: $file" exit 1 fi done

list.item: Schema-first, not code-first, when more than one client consumes the API — the SDL is the contract.
One DataLoader per relation, instantiated per-request in context. Never module-global (cross-request leak).
Cap query depth (8–10) and complexity (100–500) on every public endpoint. Tune from real traffic.
Persist queries in production: client sends a hash, server resolves to canned operation. Closes injection + DoS surface in one move.
Return `errors[].extensions.code` as a stable enum — clients should never parse `message`.
Use Relay-style cursor pagination (`first/after`, `edges`, `pageInfo`); offset pagination breaks under writes.
Field-level auth via directive (`@auth(role: ADMIN)`) or permission classes — not in business logic.
Never expose `__schema` to anonymous users in production. Disable introspection or gate on auth.
Agents love to add a new field and forget the DataLoader → silently quadratic in prod. Add a lint step that fails CI when a resolver hits the DB inside a loop.

LLM-generated SDL frequently mixes `ID` and `String`, drops `!` non-null markers, or invents `Float` for money. Pin a schema linter (`graphql-schema-linter`, `eslint-plugin-graphql`) and run before commit.
Subscription handlers are stateful — agents trained on REST will leak listeners. Require explicit `on_disconnect` cleanup in the diff.
Federation: an agent editing one subgraph can break the supergraph. Always run `rover subgraph check` against the production graph before merging — that's the human-in-loop checkpoint.
Persisted queries break iterative LLM debugging. Allow a dev-only escape hatch with header `x-persisted-bypass=1`, never in prod.

When migrating a REST endpoint to GraphQL, agents will recreate REST shapes 1:1 (one query per old endpoint). Force them to model the entity graph instead — review the type relationships, not the operation list.
Error envelopes mean a 200 OK can carry a failure. Wire your error-budget alerts on `errors[]` count, not HTTP status.

template.purpose: TypeScript DataLoader pattern: batch load + key ordering
